rounds.story: Just 40m north of the Piazza's geometric charm lies a relic of London's theatrical underbelly: the Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms. Now buried under 43 King Street's [Grade II* listed theatre](https://www.google.com/maps/search/43+King+Street/@51.5121,-0.123859,17z/), this 19th-century supper club ran 'late joys' variety shows until the 1880s, feeding punters steak and pie between music hall acts. The building originally served as Admiral Russell's Georgian townhouse before becoming a hotel - and then a venue where Dickens might've dined had he fancied a singsong with his supper. While the [National Sporting Club](https://www.google.com/maps/search/National+Sporting+Club/@51.5121,-0.1239,17z/) next door still hosts elite gentlemen, Evans' boisterous legacy lingers in the alleyways - though you'll need to imagine the raucous laughter echoing between these now-silent, heritage-listed facades.

rounds.story: Right [behind the Piazza's back wall](https://www.google.com/maps/search/Evans+Music-and-Supper+Rooms/@51.51199,-0.12387,17z/) lies a buried theatrical relic: the Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms. Though [43 King Street](https://www.google.com/maps/search/43+King+Street/@51.5121,-0.123859,17z/) now occupies the site, this 19th-century supper club ran 'Evans' Late Joys' variety shows until the 1880s, feeding punters alongside music hall acts. The original building served as Admiral Russell's Georgian townhouse before becoming a hotel - and then a venue where Dickens might've dined had he fancied a singsong with his supper. While the [National Sporting Club](https://www.google.com/maps/search/National+Sporting+Club/@51.5121,-0.1239,17z/) next door still hosts elite gentlemen, Evans' boisterous legacy lingers in the alleyways - though you'll need to imagine the raucous laughter echoing between these now-silent, heritage-listed facades.

claims.claim: Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms was located behind the Piazza's back wall.
claims.evidence: The location of Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms is shown on the map at the provided coordinates, which aligns with the description of it being behind the Piazza.

claims.claim: 43 King Street now occupies the site of the Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms.
claims.evidence: The Wikipedia article and web context both state that Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms was established in the 1840s within the former dining room of the Grand Hotel at 43 King Street.

claims.claim: The Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms ran 'Evans' Late Joys' variety shows until the 1880s.
claims.evidence: The web context from The Strange History of The Players' Theatre states that Evan's Music and Supper Rooms ran a late night variety show called Evans' Late Joys.

claims.claim: The original building served as Admiral Russell's Georgian townhouse.
claims.evidence: The Wikipedia article on Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms states that the house was built by Thomas Archer in 1712 for Admiral Edward Russell.
The web context from Evans's Supper Rooms, Covent Garden states that it became a hotel in 1772.

claims.claim: Charles Dickens might have dined at the Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms.
claims.evidence: This is a speculative claim based on the historical context of the venue, but there is no direct evidence in the provided information to confirm that Dickens actually visited.

claims.claim: The National Sporting Club still hosts elite gentlemen.
claims.evidence: The National Sporting Club is mentioned as a current entity in the evidence, and its continued operation as a club for elite gentlemen is implied by its existence and description.

claims.evidence: The evidence states that 43 King Street is a Grade II* listed building.
